ajority of Democratic voters don=E2=80=99t want Harris to run for president=
 again=E2=80=94or governor, for
that matter. In a ballot test including Harris, Echelon asked Democrats the=
ir top choice for the 2028 presidential nomination. Harris=C2=A0 came out o=
n top, the choice of 36 percent of Democrats=E2=80=94but again, that might =
also just be a function of her fame and name recognition. Many more voters=
=E2=80=9464 percent=E2=80=94wanted someone else, even if the alternatives w=
eren=E2=80=99t clear in their minds. Harris was followed in a distant secon=
d place by Pete Buttigieg (10 percent), then by Tim
Walz (9 percent), Gavin Newsom (6 percent), and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (5=
 percent).

=C2=A0

Mark Cuban and Stephen A. Smith also made the 2028 list, and, well, the two=
 media stars have to start heading to New Hampshire and South Carolina if t=
hey want to be taken seriously as candidates; Cuban was the choice of just =
3 percent of Democrats. As for Smith: For the first time in his life, he
came in as a quiet asterisk=E2=80=94an afterthought, who didn=E2=80=99t eve=
n register enough support from Democratic voters to put him ahead of =E2=80=
=9CSomeone Else,=E2=80=9D which netted 1 percent.

ong>Musk</strong>, or <strong>Trump</strong> seems like progress for the pa=

ebraska, or elsewhere from the Republicans=E2=80=99 to the Democrats=E2=80=
=99 side.=C2=A0<br/><br/><strong>Angie Craig</strong>, the Democratic ranki=

areers there can revolve around the Farm Bill=E2=80=99s (usual) five-year c=
ycle.<br/><br/>Recently, congressional dysfunction has led to yearlong exte=
nsions rather than five-year bills, and the current dysfunction may threate=
n
even <em>that</em> modest achievement. =E2=80=9CIt may be another year befo=
re it can get through,=E2=80=9D said a former member who keeps an eye on th=
